title: Fitting a bath panel with hinged access
subtitle: Build a removable bath panel that gives access to the pipework
excerpt: How to fit a front bath panel that includes a hinged or lift-out section for access to the under-bath pipework. Covers the timber frame, MDF or tile-backer panel, hinge positions, and finishing the panel edge.
sourceNotes: Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced). Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989). Trade plumbing joinery practice (UK).

glossaryTerms.definition: A simple rectangular timber frame made from 50 x 25 mm PAR (planed all round) softwood, attached to the floor and the bath rim to give the MDF panel face a rigid substrate to screw to. The frame is the structural component; the panel is the cosmetic face. Without the frame, a full-height MDF panel would flex and bow.

content.text: A bath panel with a hinged access section lets a plumber reach the trap, supply pipes, and waste connections without dismantling the panel. A standard press-fit bath panel gives access only if the panel itself is removed; a nuisance if it is painted to match the room. A hinged section, typically 400 to 500 mm wide at the trap end of the bath, opens on two or three hinges and is held shut by a magnetic catch. The frame is identical to a standard bath panel; only the panel face is divided.

50 x 25 mm planed softwood for the batten frame. 12 mm moisture-resistant MDF for the panel face. Two or three 50 mm piano hinge or butt hinges. A magnetic push catch. Stainless-steel or zinc-plated 35 mm screws. A jigsaw for cutting the MDF. A cordless drill. Waterproof primer and paint (or tiles to match the surround). Silicone sealant for the bottom edge. The tape measure, spirit level, and combination square.

Measure the height from the floor to the underside of the bath rim and the length of the front face. Cut the softwood to form a perimeter frame: a horizontal bottom batten screwed to the floor, a top batten fixed to the bath rim flange or the wall, and two vertical end pieces. Add a central vertical at the position where the access section ends; this gives both the fixed panel and the hinged section a dedicated fixing point on each side. The
should be square and rigid when complete.
Step 2, cut and fit the fixed panel

Cut the fixed section of the MDF panel (the section from the far end of the bath to the access-section cutline) using the jigsaw. Cut the access section separately. Both pieces should fit within the frame with a 2 mm clearance gap at the top and bottom edges. Prime and paint the face of both panels and the cut edges with waterproof primer and two coats of eggshell or the same finish as the room. Screw the fixed panel to the frame through the face at 300 mm centres; countersink the screws and fill the heads with flexible filler before painting.

Fit the hinges to the edge of the hinged panel first, then hold the panel up to the frame batten and mark the hinge positions on the batten. Drill pilot holes into the batten and screw the hinges into the timber. Use three hinges for a panel over 400 mm tall to prevent twist. Check that the panel opens fully without catching on the floor or the bath rim edge; trim the corner if needed. Fit the magnetic catch on the batten at the unhinged edge of the panel, and its corresponding plate on the panel face.

Apply a bead of silicone sealant along the bottom edge where the panel meets the floor, including the bottom edge of the hinged section. This prevents water splashed from the bath from running under the panel and wetting the batten frame. Do not seal the top edge; this is not a watertight joint and needs to breathe slightly to prevent condensation from rotting the timber.

attrs.symptom: Hinged section bowing or bending when opened
attrs.cause: MDF thickness insufficient (6 mm used instead of 12 mm) or too few hinges for the panel height.
attrs.fix: Add a third hinge at the midpoint of the panel. If the panel continues to bow, replace with 15 mm moisture-resistant MDF.

attrs.symptom: Panel face shows moisture damage within months
attrs.cause: Raw MDF edges exposed to bathroom humidity; primer applied only to the face, not the edges.
attrs.fix: Remove the panel, seal all four edges thoroughly with waterproof primer, allow to dry, and refit. Use moisture-resistant MDF (green board) on all future bathroom panel work.
